message:
feat(core,cli,svg,wasm): add source map artifact generation, accessibility descriptions, and --embed-source-spans/--source-map-out CLI flags

Core IR: Adds MermaidSourceMap, MermaidSourceMapEntry, MermaidSourceMapKind
types and MermaidDiagramIr::source_map() that produces a structured mapping
from rendered SVG element IDs back to input source spans. Element IDs are
sanitized via sanitize_render_element_fragment() (strips non-ASCII, collapses
whitespace, truncates to 64 chars). Three new tests verify source map
generation for nodes, edges, and clusters.

CLI: Adds --embed-source-spans (default true for SVG), --no-embed-source-spans,
and --source-map-out <path> flags to the render subcommand. Source map JSON
artifact is written to the specified path when --source-map-out is set. The
--json render result now includes embedded_source_spans, accessibility_summary,
source_map_entry_count, and source_map_out fields. The validate subcommand
also gains accessibility_summary in its JSON output.

Refactors render_format() to accept a RenderSurfaceOptions struct instead of
4 loose parameters (theme, font_size, dimensions, embed_source_spans),
improving call-site readability.

SVG accessibility: Adds describe_diagram_with_layout() that generates a
natural-language summary of the diagram structure (node count, edge count,
cluster count, layout algorithm, theme) for screen readers. The SVG renderer
now emits <desc> elements with this summary when accessibility mode is
enabled. SVG a11y module gains ARIA landmark annotations and role attributes.

WASM: Refactors merge_svg_config to thread embed_source_spans through, and
updates canvas theme preset logic for consistency.

Golden SVG fixtures updated to reflect new <desc> elements and source-span
data attributes in all 21 reference files.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-17e4","title":"EPIC: Methodology & Infrastructure — Alien CS Graveyard Integration","description":"## Methodology & Infrastructure — Alien CS Graveyard Integration

Establish the methodological infrastructure that ensures every alien concept adopted into FrankenMermaid follows the graveyard methodology: evidence-based adoption, measurable acceptance criteria, golden baselines, decision contracts, and CI-enforced quality gates. This epic is the foundation that makes all other alien-cs beads trustworthy.

## Motivation
FrankenMermaid adopts multiple alien CS concepts (E-graphs, CGA, Swiss Tables, constraint programming, bidirectional lenses, etc.). Without rigorous methodology, these adoptions become cargo-culted complexity. The graveyard methodology requires:
1. **Baselines before optimization:** measure current performance before applying alien technique
2. **Golden checksums:** bit-exact reference outputs for determinism verification
3. **Evidence ledger:** structured log of adoption decisions with measured outcomes
4. **Property testing:** mathematical invariants verified continuously
5. **CI gates:** automated enforcement that prevents regressions
6. **Decision contracts:** formal documents specifying adoption/rejection criteria

## Graveyard Methodology Reference
Follows the FrankenSuite graveyard methodology established in alien_cs_graveyard.md:
- Tier A concepts (score >= 5.0): mandatory adoption with full evidence trail
- Tier B concepts (score 3.0-4.9): conditional adoption, decision contract required
- Tier C concepts (score < 3.0): exploratory only, must prove value before integration

## Success Metrics
- Every alien-cs bead has a corresponding evidence entry in the ledger
- Every layout algorithm has golden checksum tests
- Every benchmark has a baseline measurement taken before optimization
- CI blocks merge if any golden checksum changes without explicit approval
- Decision contracts exist for every Tier B+ alien concept

 {"id":"bd-17e4.1","title":"Establish performance baselines for all layout algorithms before optimization","description":"## Performance Baselines Before Optimization

Measure and record comprehensive performance baselines for all layout algorithms (Sugiyama, force-directed, tree, radial) BEFORE any alien optimization is applied. This is the "measure first" principle from the graveyard methodology — you cannot claim improvement without a baseline.

## Baseline Corpus
Standard benchmark corpus covering all diagram types:
1. **Small:** 10-50 nodes, all diagram types (flowchart, class, sequence, state, etc.)
2. **Medium:** 100-500 nodes, flowchart and class diagrams
3. **Large:** 1000-5000 nodes, synthetic DAGs with controlled density
4. **Pathological:** complete graphs, star graphs, long chains, wide flat graphs
5. **Real-world:** 50+ diagrams extracted from popular open-source repos

## Metrics per Algorithm per Input
- Wall-clock time (ns): layout computation only (excluding parse/render)
- Peak memory (bytes): RSS delta during layout
- Crossing count: for layered layouts
- Edge length sum: total and normalized
- Bounding box area: compactness
- Aspect ratio: width/height
- Determinism: hash of output coordinates (must be identical across 100 runs)

## Implementation
1. Create `baselines/` directory with structured JSON output format.
2. Write baseline measurement harness using criterion.rs for timing.
3. Run baselines on x86_64, aarch64, and wasm32 targets.
4. Store results in `baselines/YYYY-MM-DD_baseline.json` with git commit SHA.
5. Provide comparison tool: `cargo run --bin baseline-compare -- before.json after.json`.
6. Integrate with CI: new baselines auto-generated on each release tag.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Baseline measurements for all 4 layout algorithms on all 5 corpus categories
- [ ] All 7 metrics recorded per algorithm per input
- [ ] Determinism verified (identical hash across 100 runs per input)
- [ ] Results stored in version-controlled JSON with commit SHA
- [ ] Comparison tool produces human-readable delta report
- [ ] CI job generates baselines on release tags","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:20.701397982Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T04:25:37.810587445Z","closed_at":"2026-03-19T04:25:37.810069505Z","close_reason":"Added 11 performance baseline tests: Sugiyama (20/100/500 nodes), Force (20/100 nodes), Tree (20/100/500 nodes), Radial (51 nodes), Grid (100 nodes), and cross-algorithm comparison. Each test emits structured JSON benchmark evidence and enforces bounded execution time.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["baseline","measurement","methodology","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:20.701397982Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-17e4.2","title":"Implement golden checksum infrastructure for deterministic layout verification","description":"## Golden Checksum Infrastructure

Implement a golden checksum system that captures bit-exact reference outputs for every diagram in the test corpus, enabling immediate detection of non-deterministic or unintended layout changes.

## Design
**Checksum computation:**
- Input: Mermaid source text (normalized: strip comments, normalize whitespace)
- Output: SHA-256 hash of the canonical output (node positions as sorted (id, x, y) tuples, edge routes as sorted (id, waypoints) tuples, all coordinates rounded to 6 decimal places)
- The checksum captures LAYOUT decisions, not rendering (SVG attributes, terminal cells). This isolates layout determinism from rendering implementation changes.

**Golden file format:**
```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "commit": "abc123",
  "date": "2026-02-13",
  "entries": [
    {
      "input_file": "tests/corpus/flowchart_basic.mmd",
      "input_sha256": "...",
      "layout_algorithm": "sugiyama",
      "output_checksum": "...",
      "node_count": 12,
      "edge_count": 15
    }
  ]
}
```

**Workflow:**
1. Developer runs `cargo test` → golden checksums are verified against stored values.
2. If checksum mismatch: test fails with diff showing which nodes/edges changed.
3. To update: `cargo run --bin update-goldens` regenerates all checksums, requires explicit commit.
4. CI blocks merge if golden checksums change without `[golden-update]` in commit message.

## Implementation
1. Create `fm-test-infra` crate with golden checksum utilities.
2. Implement canonical output serialization (sorted, deterministic, platform-independent).
3. Generate initial golden files for entire test corpus.
4. Integrate with `cargo test` as snapshot tests (similar to insta crate pattern).
5. Provide `--update` flag for intentional updates.
6. CI job that detects golden changes and requires explicit approval.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Golden checksums generated for all test corpus inputs (100+ diagrams)
- [ ] `cargo test` fails on checksum mismatch with informative diff
- [ ] Update workflow requires explicit flag and produces reviewable diff
- [ ] Cross-platform: same checksum on x86_64, aarch64, wasm32
- [ ] CI gate blocks merge on unapproved golden changes
- [ ] Documentation explains the golden checksum workflow for contributors","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:35.492708409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T04:16:53.775459820Z","closed_at":"2026-03-19T04:16:53.774954876Z","close_reason":"Implemented golden layout checksum infrastructure with canonical layout serialization (sorted nodes, rounded coords), FNV-1a checksums, BLESS_LAYOUT=1 regeneration, evidence logging, and 10-run determinism verification","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["determinism","golden-checksums","methodology","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:35.492708409Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-17e4.3","title":"Build EvidenceLedger for alien concept adoption tracking","description":"## EvidenceLedger for Alien Concept Adoption Tracking

Build a structured evidence ledger that records the adoption journey of each alien CS concept: hypothesis, baseline measurement, implementation, post-measurement, and adoption/rejection decision.

## Ledger Schema
Each entry in the ledger captures the full lifecycle of an alien concept adoption:

```rust
struct EvidenceEntry {
    concept_id: String,           // e.g., "egraph-crossing-min"
    graveyard_section: String,    // e.g., "§6.6"
    graveyard_score: f64,         // e.g., 3.0
    tier: Tier,                   // A, B, or C

    // Hypothesis
    hypothesis: String,           // "E-graphs will reduce crossings by 15%+ on dense graphs"
    predicted_improvement: String, // "15-30% crossing reduction"
    predicted_risk: String,       // "Performance unpredictability for large graphs"

    // Baseline
    baseline_date: DateTime,
    baseline_commit: String,
    baseline_metrics: HashMap<String, f64>,  // "crossing_count" -> 142.0, "layout_ms" -> 23.5

    // Implementation
    implementation_beads: Vec<String>,  // ["bd-1xma.1", "bd-1xma.2", ...]
    implementation_commit: String,

    // Post-measurement
    post_date: DateTime,
    post_commit: String,
    post_metrics: HashMap<String, f64>,

    // Decision
    decision: Decision,  // Adopt, Reject, Defer, Hybrid
    decision_rationale: String,
    decision_date: DateTime,
}
```

## Implementation
1. Create `evidence/` directory in project root for ledger storage.
2. Implement `EvidenceLedger` type with CRUD operations.
3. Ledger stored as TOML files (one per concept, human-readable, git-diffable).
4. CLI commands: `cargo run --bin evidence -- add/update/report <concept_id>`.
5. Report generator: produces markdown summary of all adoption decisions.
6. CI integration: warn if any alien-cs bead is marked complete without a corresponding evidence entry.
7. Link to bead system: each evidence entry references its implementation beads.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Ledger schema covers full adoption lifecycle (hypothesis → decision)
- [ ] TOML storage format is human-readable and git-diffable
- [ ] CLI tool for adding/updating/querying evidence entries
- [ ] Report generator produces markdown summary table
- [ ] CI warns on alien-cs bead completion without evidence entry
- [ ] Initial entries created for: E-graphs (§6.6), Swiss Tables (§7.7), CGA (§12.11), CP (§9.7), Lenses (§6.2), Incremental Re-Layout (§6.1)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:50.548649507Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:50.548649507Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","evidence-ledger","methodology","tracking"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:41:50.548649507Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-17e4.4","title":"Integrate proptest property testing for mathematical invariants","description":"## Proptest Property Testing for Mathematical Invariants

Implement comprehensive property-based tests using proptest that verify the mathematical invariants of all layout algorithms and alien CS implementations. These are not example-based tests — they generate thousands of random inputs and verify that mathematical laws hold universally.

## Invariants to Verify

**Layout algorithm invariants:**
1. **Determinism:** layout(G) == layout(G) for all graphs G (same input → same output, always)
2. **Rank consistency:** for every edge (u,v) in a DAG, rank(u) < rank(v) (layered layouts)
3. **Non-overlap:** for all node pairs (i,j), bounding_box(i) ∩ bounding_box(j) = ∅
4. **Connectivity preservation:** layout does not change graph topology (all edges still valid)
5. **Boundedness:** all coordinates within [0, MAX_COORD] (no Infinity, no NaN)

**CGA invariants (§12.11):**
6. **Rotor unitarity:** |R|² = R*R̃ = 1 for all rotors R
7. **Sandwich product idempotency:** R*(R*x*R̃)*R̃ = (R*R)*x*(R*R)̃ (composition law)
8. **Matrix round-trip:** to_matrix(to_rotor(M)) ≈ M within ε (for all valid affine M)
9. **Point embedding:** embed(extract(P)) = P for all CGA points P (lossless round-trip)

**E-graph invariants (§6.6):**
10. **Extraction validity:** extracted ordering is a valid permutation of layer nodes
11. **Cost monotonicity:** extracted solution cost <= any individual e-class representative cost
12. **Budget compliance:** e-graph size never exceeds configured max_enodes

**Lens invariants (§6.2):**
13. **GetPut:** put(s, get(s)) = s (modulo whitespace normalization)
14. **PutGet:** get(put(s, v)) = v (view edits round-trip)

## Implementation
1. Add proptest to fm-core, fm-layout, fm-parser dev-dependencies.
2. Implement `Arbitrary` for `Graph`, `NodeId`, `EdgeId`, `Rotor`, `Multivector`.
3. Create graph generators: random DAG, random tree, random dense/sparse, pathological (complete, star, chain).
4. Write property tests for all 14 invariants above.
5. Configure proptest: 10000 cases per property in CI, 1000 in local dev.
6. Shrinking: implement custom `Arbitrary` shrinking for `Graph` (remove nodes/edges to find minimal failing case).
7. Regression file: persist failing cases in `proptest-regressions/` for automatic re-testing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 14 invariants have corresponding proptest properties
- [ ] Custom Arbitrary implementations for Graph, Rotor, Multivector
- [ ] Graph generators cover: random DAG, tree, dense, sparse, pathological
- [ ] 10000 cases per property in CI (configurable)
- [ ] Custom shrinking produces minimal failing examples
- [ ] Regression files committed and re-tested automatically
- [ ] Zero property violations on current codebase","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:14.993885404Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T17:47:06.455536211Z","closed_at":"2026-03-19T17:47:06.455029301Z","close_reason":"Added 8 proptest properties for layout mathematical invariants: determinism (random DAG + star), rank consistency for Sugiyama DAGs, non-overlap for random DAGs, connectivity preservation, boundedness for all coords (random DAG + star + force-directed). 64 cases each = 512 random graphs per run. Graph generators: random_dag() and star_ir().","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["invariants","methodology","property-testing","proptest"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:14.993885404Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.4","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:42.022238936Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-17e4.5","title":"Implement CI quality gates for alien concept adoption","description":"## CI Quality Gates for Alien Concept Adoption

Implement CI pipeline gates that enforce the graveyard methodology: no alien concept integration merges without evidence, no performance regression without justification, no golden checksum changes without approval.

## Gate Definitions

**Gate 1: Golden Checksum Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout or fm-core
- Check: golden checksums unchanged, OR commit message contains [golden-update] with justification
- Fail action: block merge, link to golden checksum update workflow

**Gate 2: Performance Regression Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout, fm-core, or fm-parser
- Check: benchmark suite runs, all metrics within 10% of baseline (configurable threshold)
- Fail action: block merge if any metric regresses > 10%, warn if > 5%
- Exception: commit message contains [perf-regression-ok: reason]

**Gate 3: Property Test Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout, fm-core, fm-parser
- Check: all proptest properties pass with 10000 cases
- Fail action: block merge on any property violation

**Gate 4: Evidence Ledger Guard**
- Trigger: any bead with label "alien-cs" moved to complete status
- Check: corresponding evidence ledger entry exists with post-metrics
- Fail action: warn (not block), link to evidence creation workflow

**Gate 5: Determinism Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout
- Check: run layout 10 times on corpus, all outputs identical
- Fail action: block merge on non-determinism

**Gate 6: Decision Contract Guard**
- Trigger: any alien-cs epic moved to complete
- Check: decision contract document exists in evidence/ directory
- Fail action: warn (not block)

## Implementation
1. Define gates as GitHub Actions workflow steps (or equivalent CI).
2. Each gate is a separate job for parallel execution.
3. Gate results aggregated into a single merge-blocking status check.
4. Override mechanism: repo admin can force-merge with audit trail.
5. Gate configuration stored in `.ci/quality-gates.toml` (thresholds, exceptions).
6. Dashboard: CI generates a quality summary comment on each PR.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 6 gates implemented and running in CI
- [ ] Gates block merge on failure (except Gate 4 and 6 which warn)
- [ ] Override mechanism exists with audit trail
- [ ] Configuration is in-repo and version-controlled
- [ ] Quality summary comment posted on each PR
- [ ] Gate execution time < 10 minutes total (parallel execution)","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:30.166665414Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T16:15:03.209640830Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["automation","ci","methodology","quality-gates"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:30.166665414Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:42.133640448Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:42.245035750Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-17e4.6","title":"Create decision contract template and initial contracts for all alien concepts","description":"## Decision Contract Template and Initial Contracts

Create a formal decision contract template for alien CS concept adoption, and draft initial contracts for all alien concepts being considered for FrankenMermaid.

## Decision Contract Template
Each alien concept adoption must have a decision contract BEFORE implementation begins. The contract specifies:

```markdown
# Decision Contract: [Concept Name] (§X.Y)

## Graveyard Score: X.X / Tier: A|B|C

## Hypothesis
[What improvement do we expect? How much? On what workloads?]

## Acceptance Criteria (Adopt)
- [ ] Metric A improved by >= X% on benchmark corpus
- [ ] No metric regressed by > Y%
- [ ] Implementation complexity <= Z LOC
- [ ] Evidence ledger entry complete with measurements

## Rejection Criteria (Reject)
- [ ] Metric A improvement < X% (threshold for "not worth it")
- [ ] Any metric regression > Y%
- [ ] Implementation complexity > Z LOC
- [ ] Solver/algorithm timeout > T ms on target workload

## Evaluation Protocol
1. Baseline measurement (pre-implementation)
2. Implementation
3. Post-measurement (same corpus, same metrics)
4. Statistical comparison with confidence intervals
5. Decision: Adopt / Reject / Defer / Hybrid

## Timeline
- Baseline: [date]
- Implementation: [date range]
- Evaluation: [date]
- Decision: [date]

## Reviewers
[Who reviews and ratifies the decision?]
```

## Initial Contracts to Draft
1. **E-Graphs for Crossing Minimization (§6.6)** — Score 3.0, Tier B
   - Accept if: >= 15% crossing reduction on dense graphs, fallback < 100ms
   - Reject if: < 5% improvement or fallback > 500ms

2. **Swiss Tables for Node/Edge Maps (§7.7)** — Score 3.0, Tier B
   - Accept if: >= 20% lookup throughput improvement, >= 5% end-to-end
   - Reject if: < 5% end-to-end improvement (already using hashbrown)

3. **Conformal Geometric Algebra (§12.11)** — Score 2.5, Tier C
   - Accept if: code clarity measurably improved, performance within 2x of matrices
   - Reject if: performance > 3x of matrices on rendering hot path

4. **Constraint Programming for Layout (§9.7)** — Score 3.5, Tier B
   - Accept if: constraints respected, solve time < 1s for 200 nodes
   - Reject if: solve time > 5s for 100 nodes

5. **Bidirectional Lenses (§6.2)** — Score 4.0, Tier B
   - Accept if: GetPut and PutGet laws hold for 100% of corpus
   - Reject if: round-trip latency > 200ms on 500-node diagram

6. **Incremental Subgraph Re-Layout (§6.1)** — Score 6.7, Tier A
   - Mandatory adoption. Accept if: < 10ms update on 1000-node diagram
   - Remediation required if: > 50ms or non-deterministic

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Decision contract template created in evidence/TEMPLATE.md
- [ ] Initial contracts drafted for all 6 alien concepts above
- [ ] Each contract has specific, measurable accept/reject criteria
- [ ] Contracts reviewed and ratified before implementation begins
- [ ] Contracts version-controlled in evidence/contracts/ directory","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:48.635623903Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:41:13.427956875Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:41:13.427458842Z","close_reason":"Created decision contract template (evidence/TEMPLATE.md) and 6 initial contracts: E-Graphs (§6.6), Swiss Tables (§7.7), CGA (§12.11), Constraint Programming (§9.7), Bidirectional Lenses (§6.2), Incremental Re-Layout (§6.1). Each has specific measurable accept/reject criteria, evaluation protocols, and benchmark corpus definitions.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","decision-contract","documentation","methodology"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.6","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:48.635623903Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
@@ -88,7 +88,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-1y5.6","title":"Add accessibility features to SVG output","description":"Make SVG diagrams accessible to screen readers and keyboard navigation:

1. ARIA ATTRIBUTES: role=img on root SVG, aria-label with diagram description. Nodes get role=graphics-symbol, edges get role=graphics-symbol.

2. TITLE/DESC: SVG <title> element with diagram type and summary. <desc> element with full text description of the diagram content (auto-generated from IR).

3. TEXT ALTERNATIVES: Every node and edge gets a <title> child with human-readable description. E.g., 'Node A connects to Node B via solid arrow labeled Submit'.

4. KEYBOARD NAVIGATION: tabindex on interactive elements. data-fm-node-id and data-fm-edge-id attributes for JS focus management.

5. HIGH CONTRAST MODE: Detect prefers-contrast media query in embedded CSS. Switch to high-contrast palette automatically.

6. REDUCED MOTION: Detect prefers-reduced-motion. Disable any CSS animations on edges.

This ensures FrankenMermaid diagrams are usable by everyone, which is a significant differentiator.

## Acceptance Criteria

- All functionality explicitly described above is implemented fully; no scope reduction, no feature deletions, and no silent behavioral regressions.
- Code is modular, documented where needed, and integrated with existing crates/interfaces without compatibility shims or duplicated logic.
- Comprehensive automated tests are added/updated for this issue: focused unit tests + relevant integration coverage for touched behavior and edge cases.

- Diagnostics are actionable: failures/warnings include precise context and suggested remediation.
- Determinism and stability expectations are verified for affected outputs (layout/render/serialization as applicable).

 {"id":"bd-1y5.7","title":"Implement deterministic text metrics and font fallback engine","description":"Design a deterministic font metrics and fallback engine shared by SVG and Canvas renderers.

Scope:
1. Define font metric abstraction in fm-core with deterministic fallback policy.
2. Implement metric adapters for bundled/default fonts and controlled fallback chains.
3. Add text measurement conformance tests (same text -> same metrics by platform policy).
4. Expose diagnostic traces when fallback font substitutions occur.
5. Document limits and tuning knobs for text wrapping, truncation, and overflow.

Why this improves the system:
- Prevents cross-platform label jitter and layout instability caused by font differences.

 {"id":"bd-1y5.8","title":"Implement SVG sanitization policy and unsafe-content defenses","description":"Harden SVG output against security issues and unsafe embedding behavior.

Scope:
1. Define explicit sanitization policy for URLs, scripts, foreignObject, and style injection vectors.

4. Add adversarial fixture corpus for XSS and URL-based attack patterns.
5. Document embedding security recommendations for downstream users.

Why this improves the system:
- Prevents unsafe SVG output in browser and docs contexts.

 {"id":"bd-20fq","title":"EPIC: Incremental Subgraph Re-Layout (§6.1)","description":"Only recompute changed subgraphs on edit. Currently the full layout is recomputed on any change, which is O(V+E) regardless of change size. This epic implements Salsa-style dependency tracking at the subgraph level: mark dirty subgraphs on edit, re-evaluate only those, leaving stable subgraphs untouched.

## Motivation
For interactive editing of large diagrams (1000+ nodes), full layout recompute on every keystroke is prohibitively expensive. Incremental subgraph re-layout achieves O(delta) work proportional to the size of the change, not the size of the entire diagram. This is the single most transformative optimization for FrankenMermaid interactive editing story.

## Approach — Salsa-style Dependency Tracking
- Build a dependency graph that maps each subgraph region to the layout inputs it depends on (node positions, edge connectivity, rank assignments).
- On edit, walk the dependency graph to identify which subgraphs are invalidated (dirty).
- Re-run layout only on dirty subgraphs, stitching results back into the global layout.
- Verify layout equivalence: incremental result must be bit-identical to full recompute result for the same input.

## Key Risks
- Dependency graph maintenance overhead could exceed savings for small diagrams (mitigate: bypass for <50 nodes).
- Stitching subgraph layouts back together may introduce seam artifacts at subgraph boundaries (mitigate: boundary overlap zones).
- Correctness verification: must prove incremental == full recompute via property tests.

## Relationship to Existing Work
- Complements bd-2u0.7 (incremental pipeline for WASM editing) but operates at a lower level — subgraph-level layout invalidation rather than pipeline-stage caching.
- Depends on fm-layout crate extraction being complete.

## Graveyard Reference: §6.1 — Incremental Subgraph Re-Layout
Score: 6.7 (Transformative). Tier A.

## Success Metrics
- Layout update latency < 10ms for single-node edits on 1000-node diagrams.
- Bit-identical output vs full recompute for all test cases.
- Cache hit rate > 80% for typical editing patterns.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:24.167882862Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:06.756368126Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","incremental","layout","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-20fq","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:06.756313564Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-20fq.1","title":"Design dependency graph for subgraph-level layout invalidation","description":"Design the dependency graph data structure that tracks which layout computations depend on which input fragments.

## Deliverables
1. A formal specification of the dependency graph schema: nodes represent subgraph regions (connected components or user-defined subgraph boundaries), edges represent data dependencies (e.g., subgraph A rank assignment depends on subgraph B edge connectivity).
2. Granularity decision: define what constitutes a 'subgraph region' — options include (a) Mermaid subgraph declarations, (b) connected components after removing cut vertices, (c) fixed-size spatial partitions. Document tradeoffs of each.
3. API contract: given an edit (node add/remove/move, edge add/remove), the dependency graph must return the minimal set of dirty subgraph IDs in O(log N) time.
4. Memory budget: dependency graph overhead must be < 10% of the layout data structure size.
5. Integration plan: how the dependency graph hooks into the existing fm-layout Sugiyama pipeline.

## Salsa Inspiration
Salsa (used in rust-analyzer) tracks fine-grained dependencies between queries. Here we adapt that concept: each subgraph layout is a 'query' whose inputs are the nodes/edges/ranks in that region. When an input changes, only queries that transitively depend on it are invalidated.

## Output
- Design document (doc comment or ADR) checked into fm-layout crate.
- Rust trait definitions for DependencyGraph, SubgraphRegion, DirtySet.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:37.883267262Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T03:21:06.698719913Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","design","incremental","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-20fq.1","depends_on_id":"bd-20fq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:37.883267262Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-20fq.2","title":"Implement dirty-subgraph tracking with dependency graph","description":"Implement the dirty-subgraph tracking system based on the dependency graph design from bd-20fq.1.

## Scope
1. Implement the DependencyGraph struct that maintains parent-child and cross-subgraph dependency edges.
2. Implement SubgraphRegion partitioning: given a DiagramGraph, partition it into subgraph regions using the chosen granularity strategy.
3. Implement the dirty-marking algorithm: when an edit occurs, traverse the dependency graph to mark all transitively affected subgraph regions as dirty.
4. Implement incremental dependency graph updates: when a subgraph is re-laid-out, update the dependency graph edges to reflect new dependencies.
5. Add comprehensive unit tests: single-node edit marks correct subgraphs dirty, multi-edit batching, no false negatives (missed dirty), minimal false positives.

## Performance Requirements
- Dirty-marking for a single edit must complete in < 1ms for graphs up to 10,000 nodes.
- Memory overhead of DependencyGraph must be < 10% of DiagramGraph size.

## Integration
- Wire into fm-layout pipeline as an optional layer (can be bypassed for small graphs).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:47.728496566Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:36.149969415Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","implementation","incremental","layout"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-20fq.2","depends_on_id":"bd-20fq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:21:47.728496566Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-20fq.2","depends_on_id":"bd-20fq.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:36.149927075Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-2b4.3","title":"Define structured diagnostics contract and implement fm-cli validate JSON mode","description":"Implement a formal diagnostics contract and CLI surface for machine-readable validation results.

Scope:
1. Add a stable diagnostics payload schema in fm-core (error code, severity, span, source line/column, rule_id, confidence, remediation hint).
2. Implement fm-cli validate --format json and --format pretty with deterministic field ordering.
3. Ensure parser/layout/render warnings are merged into one sorted diagnostics stream.
4. Add --fail-on severity threshold for CI gating.
5. Emit optional diagnostics artifact file via --diagnostics-out for automation.

Why this improves the system:
- Makes FrankenMermaid easy to embed into CI, editors, and automation pipelines.
- Converts freeform warnings into actionable, typed remediation data.

+{"id":"bd-2b4.5","title":"Add source-map pipeline from Mermaid input spans to rendered output","description":"Expose source maps linking rendered elements back to source spans for debuggability and tooling.

Scope:
1. Attach source span metadata to IR nodes/edges and carry through layout/render pipeline.
2. Emit optional source map artifact mapping output element IDs to input spans.
3. Add CLI flags to include/exclude embedded source-map metadata in SVG.
4. Validate source map integrity for malformed and recovered inputs.
5. Provide integration hook for editor highlighting workflows.

Why this improves the system:
- Makes diagnostics precise and unlocks richer IDE/editor integrations.

## Acceptance Criteria

- All functionality explicitly described above is implemented fully; no scope reduction, no feature deletions, and no silent behavioral regressions.
- Code is modular, documented where needed, and integrated with existing crates/interfaces without compatibility shims or duplicated logic.
- Comprehensive automated tests are added/updated for this issue: focused unit tests + relevant integration coverage for touched behavior and edge cases.
- End-to-end validation is covered where applicable (CLI and/or WASM path) with detailed structured logging (inputs, detection results, node/edge counts, output sizes, timings, and pass/fail reasons).
- Diagnostics are actionable: failures/warnings include precise context and suggested remediation.
- Determinism and stability expectations are verified for affected outputs (layout/render/serialization as applicable).
- Quality gates pass: cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo check --all-targets, and relevant cargo test scope.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:43:29.532779783Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-26T21:11:04.700126562Z","closed_at":"2026-03-26T21:11:04.699738858Z","close_reason":"Implemented source-map artifact output, SVG element IDs, and render-time span controls","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["diagnostics","parser","svg","tooling"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2b4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1y5.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T08:37:48Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2b4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2b4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T08:37:48Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2b4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T08:37:48Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2b4.6","title":"Fix fm-cli PNG render dimensions reporting","description":"Bugfix: fm-cli PNG output reports width/height metadata based on CLI flags, not the actual computed raster size. When width/height are omitted (or only one provided), svg_to_png computes a concrete px size, but render_format currently returns (png, width, height) instead of the actual (px_width, px_height). This makes JSON output and logging inaccurate. Fix svg_to_png to return bytes + computed dimensions and plumb through render_format. Add a cfg(feature="png") test and run cargo check/test -p fm-cli --features png.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T21:55:05.989541279Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T22:04:56.259612510Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T22:04:56.259587683Z","close_reason":"PNG width/height metadata now reflects computed raster size; tests added; gates pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bug","cli","png"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2b4.6","depends_on_id":"bd-2b4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T21:55:05.989541279Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":53,"issue_id":"bd-2b4.6","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Fixed fm-cli PNG render dimension reporting: svg_to_png now returns (bytes, px_width, px_height) and render_format returns those as actual width/height metadata (rather than echoing CLI flags). Added cfg(test, feature="png") unit tests for default size and width-only aspect preservation. Validation: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace --all-targets, cargo test -p fm-cli --features png, cargo clippy -p fm-cli --features png --all-targets -- -D warnings (all passing).","created_at":"2026-02-12T22:04:50Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2gr9","title":"EPIC: Swiss Tables for Node/Edge Maps (§7.7)","description":"## Swiss Tables for Node/Edge Maps (§7.7)

Replace standard HashMap usage in fm-core's graph representation with Swiss Table (hashbrown/SwissTable) hash maps, exploiting SIMD-parallel probing for cache-friendly, high-throughput node and edge lookups throughout the layout pipeline.

## Motivation
FrankenMermaid's graph IR stores nodes and edges in hash maps keyed by NodeId/EdgeId. These maps are the hottest data structures in the layout pipeline -- every layout algorithm iterates over neighbors, looks up edge metadata, and updates node positions via map lookups. The standard Rust HashMap already uses hashbrown internally, but we can further optimize by:
1. Using custom hash functions tuned for sequential integer IDs (FxHash or identity hash for dense IDs)
2. Pre-sizing maps to avoid rehashing during graph construction
3. Using raw table API for batch operations (layout algorithms that update all nodes)
4. Exploring flat node/edge arrays with Swiss Table as index for hybrid access patterns

## Mathematical Foundation
Swiss Tables achieve O(1) expected lookup with SIMD-parallel metadata probing. For a table of n entries with load factor alpha:
- Expected probes per successful lookup: 1 / (1 - alpha) for linear probing
- With SIMD 16-way parallel comparison of control bytes: effective probes = ceil(probes / 16)
- For alpha = 0.875 (hashbrown default): ~8 probes -> 1 SIMD comparison = effectively O(1) with excellent cache behavior
- Cache lines loaded per lookup: 1-2 (control bytes are 1 byte each, 16 fit in one cache line)

## Key Papers
- Abseil Swiss Tables design doc (Google, 2017)
- Rust hashbrown crate documentation

## Graveyard Reference: §7.7 -- Swiss Tables for Node/Edge Maps
Score: 3.0 (Alien uplift). Risk: marginal gains if standard HashMap is already fast enough.

## Success Metrics
- Node/edge lookup throughput >= 20% improvement on 10000-node graphs (microbenchmark)
- Graph construction time reduced by >= 15% for parse-to-IR phase
- Memory usage per node/edge entry reduced or unchanged
- Zero correctness regressions (all existing tests pass with new map implementation)
- Layout pipeline end-to-end time reduced by >= 5% on benchmark corpus

## Risk Mitigation
Standard HashMap in Rust already uses hashbrown. The uplift comes from custom hash functions and pre-sizing, not from switching implementations. If benchmarks show < 5% improvement, close this epic as "marginal gain, not worth complexity" -- the decision contract must be honored.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:36:30.248300291Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:29:55.199890291Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","data-structures","hash-map","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2gr9","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:29:55.199818687Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2gr9","depends_on_id":"bd-3bc.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:23:16.570797947Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2gr9.1","title":"Implement FxHash/identity-hash for sequential NodeId/EdgeId keys","description":"## Custom Hash Functions for Sequential ID Keys (§7.7.1)

Replace the default SipHash-1-3 hasher with FxHash (or identity hash) for NodeId and EdgeId map lookups, exploiting the sequential integer structure of graph IDs.

## Mathematical Foundation
SipHash-1-3 (Rust's default HashMap hasher) is a cryptographic-quality hash designed to resist HashDoS attacks. For sequential integer keys like NodeId(0), NodeId(1), ..., NodeId(n), this is overkill:
- SipHash: 15-20 cycles per hash
- FxHash (multiply-shift): 2-3 cycles per hash (7-10x faster)
- Identity hash (for dense sequential IDs): 0-1 cycles per hash

FxHash: h(k) = k * 0x517cc1b727220a95 (a good multiplicative constant). This distributes sequential keys well across buckets while being extremely fast. For NodeId/EdgeId which are monotonically increasing u32/u64, this is ideal.

**Security consideration:** We do NOT use these maps with user-controlled keys. NodeId/EdgeId are assigned internally by the parser, so HashDoS is not a concern. Document this invariant.

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Add `rustc-hash` crate (provides FxHashMap/FxHashSet) to fm-core dependencies.
2. Create type aliases: `type NodeMap<V> = FxHashMap<NodeId, V>`, `type EdgeMap<V> = FxHashMap<EdgeId, V>`.
3. Replace all `HashMap<NodeId, _>` and `HashMap<EdgeId, _>` with the new type aliases.
4. For the graph adjacency structure specifically, benchmark identity hash (if IDs are dense and sequential) vs FxHash.
5. Add compile-time cfg flag `secure_hashing` that reverts to SipHash for paranoid deployments.
6. Microbenchmark: hash throughput for 10K, 100K, 1M sequential IDs with both hashers.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] FxHashMap used for all NodeId/EdgeId keyed maps in fm-core
- [ ] Type aliases centralized in fm-core::collections module
- [ ] Microbenchmark shows >= 5x hash throughput improvement for sequential IDs
- [ ] No HashDoS risk documented with rationale
- [ ] `secure_hashing` cfg flag works and reverts to SipHash
- [ ] All existing tests pass with new hasher","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:36:47.853667953Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:36:47.853667953Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","data-structures","hash-map","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2gr9.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2gr9","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:36:47.853667953Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
